segments.s276-g1: This opening chunk strings together several modifiers before the verb in the next segment. و is the coordinating particle 'and.' دیگر here means 'another' or 'again' (an additional item). گروی is an archaic/uncertain form that in this context functions as a noun modifying زره (it is not the verbal sense given in the lexicon and so likely a poetic or dialectal noun); زره دیو is the noun phrase 'the demon's armor' (زره = armor, دیو = demon). نیو appears as a proper name attached to the demon (an epithet), not the listed meanings 'ney' or 'new.'
segments.s276-g2: برون‌رفت here functions as a past action 'came/forth went out' (classical compound of برون + رفت) — the lexicon entries given are nominal but the sense in the line is verbal: 'came out' or 'appeared.' با is the instrumental/comitative 'with.' پور is the title/element meaning 'son (of)' that precedes the father's name; گودرز is the father's name and گیو is the son's (Giv), ordered as 'with Giv, son of Goudarz.'
segments.s276-g3: به marks instrument or means here ('with' / 'by'), followed by نیزه فراوان 'many / abundant spears' (نیزه = spear; فراوان as adjective/adverb 'abundant' or 'in great numbers'). برآویختند is a 3rd-person plural past verb meaning 'they sprang upon' or 'they leapt/attacked' — a vigorous, sudden assault.
segments.s276-g4: همی is the old epic particle that gives an ongoing/intensive flavor to the action (an intensifier here). زهر با خون is a simple coordinated noun phrase 'poison with blood' (زهر = poison; با = with; خون = blood). برآمیختند is the plural past 'they mixed / mingled,' so the clause means 'they mingled poison with blood.'
segments.s276-g5: سناندار is an adjective/noun 'spear-bearing' or 'the spear-bearer' (used as a nominal epithet). نیزه names the weapon (spear). ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from' or 'out of.' چنگ سوار here means 'the rider's grasp/hand' — چنگ in ordinary glosses can mean 'claw' or 'harp', but in the phrase it denotes a hand/grip (poetic usage), and سوار = rider/horseman; so ز چنگ سوار = 'from the rider's hold.'
segments.s276-g6: فرو ریخت is a compound expressing a downward falling or collapse: فرو 'down(ward)' + ریخت (here verbal 'fell, collapsed') — together 'plunged/fell down.' از هول = 'because of/from the terror' (از + هول where از carries the causal 'because of' sense). آن کارزار = 'that battlefield/struggle.' The whole clause describes a fall caused by fear on that field.
segments.s276-g7: کمان is 'bow' (weapon). برگرفتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they took up / lifted' (بر + گرفتند). و is the coordinating 'and.' تیر خدنگ is a compound noun: تیر 'arrow' and خدنگ 'short sword/dagger' used here to describe a particular kind of missile or stabbing projectile (a dagger-like arrow).
segments.s276-g8: یک اندر دگر is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'one against another' or 'one after another / at each other.' تاخته is the past participial/verb form 'rushed/charged' (they rushed). چون is the comparative 'like,' and پلنگ is the simile 'leopard' — together 'they rushed at one another like a leopard.' Word order follows classical Persian where the verb (تاخته) closes the clause.
segments.s276-g25: نشست از بر زین is a compact, somewhat archaic phrasing meaning 'he sat down from upon the saddle' = 'he dismounted.' از بر is a poetic double preposition (از + بر) meaning 'off/of from upon.' او را is marked by the accusative particle را and is the object that will be realized by the verb that follows; به پیش is an adverbial ('toward the front/ahead') that links to the following verb.
segments.s276-g26: دوانید here is a past, causative/agentive sense 'made/put (someone) to run' or 'drove him on' — it takes the previously marked object او را and the direction به پیش. شد تا is an idiomatic sequence where شد (past of شدن) indicates movement/progression ('he proceeded') and تا introduces the limit 'until.' بر + یار means 'to/upon the companion,' and the possessive suffix ش on خوی marks 'his' (خوی + ش = خویش 'his own').
segments.s276-g27: به بالا برآمد is 'he rose up (to a high place)' — برآمد is past tense 'arose/ascended.' درفشی به دست is an instrumental phrase 'a banner in (his) hand' (درفشی = banner/standard, and به دست = 'in hand/with'). Word order places the locative/height phrase before the verb in classical style.
segments.s276-g28: به نعره here is instrumental 'with a shout' (به + noun = 'by/with'); همی is the archaic/poetic particle marking continuous or emphatic action ('then/withal/so he...'). کوه را کرد پست is a causative clause: را marks کوه as definite object, کرد 'made/did' and پست is the predicative adjective 'low' — literally 'he caused the mountain to be low' (hyperbolic praise/effect).
segments.s276-g29: به پیروزی شاه ایران‌زمین is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for/on the victory of the king of the land of Iran' — به marks the theme/purpose ('in honor of/for'), پیروزی is 'victory,' and ایران‌زمین is a fixed compound meaning 'Iranian realm.'
segments.s276-g30: همی خواند is iterative/continuous past 'he kept calling/singing'; خواند here means 'uttered/sang/cried out' (performed the exclamation). بر پهلوان آفرین is the content/direction of the cry: بر = 'toward/for', پهلوان = 'champion/hero', and آفرین is the acclamation 'bravo' — the whole = 'he cried "Bravo to the hero."'
segments.s276-g17: عمودی is an adverbial/qualifying word placed before the past-tense verb بزد to mark the manner of the blow ('vertical/straight down'). بزد is the simple past ('he struck'). بر + سر is a prepositional object ('upon the head'); the coordinating و links that object to ترگ اوی, where اوی is an enclitic possessive ('his'), so ترگ اوی = 'his ترگ' functions as a second object of the verb.
segments.s276-g18: که introduces a result/explanatory clause ('so that/that'). خون اندر آمد is a literary phrasing where اندر آمد (past) means 'came/appeared' or 'ran forth' (here 'blood ran out'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; تارک would be the source ('the crown/top of the head') but is an archaic/uncertain form; به + روی indicates direction 'to the face'.
segments.s276-g19: همیدون is a proper name placed before the verb as subject. ز زین is a prepositional source phrase ('from the saddle'). دست بگذاردش is a verbal phrase in which دست functions as the instrumental/handling noun and بگذاردش (verb + object enclitic ش) means 'put/removed/placed him' — here 'took him (off) by the hand/from the saddle.'
segments.s276-g20: گرفتش is a past-tense verb with enclitic object ش ('he seized him'). به بر is a locative/directional phrase here meaning 'to/against his chest' (به + بر used together); سخت functions adverbially ('firmly/strongly'). The coordinating و links the following verb بفشاردش ('and he squeezed him'), another past/preterite verb with the object enclitic.
segments.s276-g21: که introduces this clause ('so/that'). بر پشت زین is a locative phrase ('on the back of the saddle') placed before the noun phrase. مرد is the noun 'the man' (subject), followed by بی‌توش, an archaic/adjectival predicate (uncertain meaning here), and گشت is the past copular/transformative verb 'became/was turned', expressing a change of state.
segments.s276-g22: ز اسب اندر افتاد is an idiomatic poetic phrasing meaning 'fell from the horse' (ز = 'from', اندر افتاد = 'fell down/ off'). The clause is then coordinated with و + predicate: بیهوش گشت ('and he became unconscious'), where بیهوش is a predicative adjective and گشت is the past copula 'became'.
segments.s276-g23: فرود آمد is a past verb meaning 'alighted/descended'. In poetic word order the verb appears before its nominal subject; از باره جنگی is an ablative/temporal phrase ('on the occasion of war' / 'in the course of battle') modifying the arrival, and پلنگ (panther/leopard) is the subject appearing after these modifiers for emphasis.
segments.s276-g24: دو دست is an instrumental phrase 'with both hands'. از پس پشت is a spatial phrase 'from behind the back' (از = 'from', پس used here as 'behind'). بستش is a past-tense verb with enclitic ش meaning 'he bound/seized him'. چو سنگ is a simile 'like a stone' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s276-g9: همی is an adverb (here ‘still/continually’) placed before the predicate for emphasis; زنده is a predicative adjective ‘alive’. بایست is a past/stative verbal form meaning ‘stood/was kept’ (here ‘was kept/left’). مر is an archaic first‑person clitic (= “my / to me”) attached before the name, and گیو را is the noun plus the object marker را, so the whole segment reads literally ‘continually alive stood my Giv’ → ‘he kept my Giv alive’.
segments.s276-g10: کز is the subordinating/relative particle ‘that/for/for he who’ (introducing the reason/clause). اسب اندر آرد is a verb phrase: اسب ‘horse’ + اندر آرد (آوردن) ‘brings/pulls (into or off)’, and گو نیو را is treated here as a personal name (گو‑نیو) with the object marker را. Grammatically the clause gives the cause: ‘for he pulled Go‑Niu from/off the horse.’
segments.s276-g11: چنان بسته is an adjectival phrase ‘thus/so bound/tied’ placed before the verb for descriptive effect. در پیش خسرو برد is a verb phrase in which در پیش + proper name خسرو gives the location/scene ‘in the presence of the king’; برد here functions as a past verbal ‘carried/took’ (classical Persian often uses برد for ‘took/led’). The overall order is descriptive phrase + location + verb.
segments.s276-g12: ز ترکان یکی هدیه نو برد is a short clause giving provenance and object: ز ترکان ‘from the Turks’ (prepositional phrase), یکی ‘a certain/one’ qualifies هدیه ‘a gift’, نو ‘new’ modifies هدیه, and برد again is the past verb ‘brought/took’. Word order is prepositional phrase → quantifier/adjective → noun → verb.
segments.s276-g13: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’; گیو اندر آمد is the main temporal clause ‘Giv came in/entered’ (اندر آمد = ‘entered/came in’). گروی از نهیب is a short phrase where از نهیب ‘from/by the shout/clamor’ gives the cause or accompaniment; گروی here indicates the reaction/state connected to that shout. The sequence is temporal clause → circumstance/cause.
segments.s276-g14: کمان شد ز دستش به سوی نشیب: کمان شد is a light‑verb construction with شد ‘became/happened’ meaning the bow was displaced; ز دستش ‘from his hand’ indicates source (‘fell out of his hand’), به سوی ‘toward’ marks direction, and نشیب ‘the slope/descent’ is the goal. Classical Persian often uses شد + noun to mark an intransitive change of state.
segments.s276-g15: سوی تیغ برد آن زمان دست خویش is organized as directional phrase → verb → temporal phrase → possessed noun: سوی تیغ برد ‘he put/took (his hand) toward the sword’ (برد used as past verb ‘took/moved’), آن زمان ‘at that time’ gives the temporal frame, and دست خویش ‘his own hand’ is the moving agent (خویش formed from خوی + possessive ش). Note classical word order allows these modifiers before/after the verb.
segments.s276-g16: دمان گیو نیو اندر آمد به پیش: دمان ‘hem/skirt’ (or ‘lower part’) + a proper name compound گیو نیو (here treated as a unit) form the noun phrase ‘the hem of Giv‑Niu’; اندر آمد ‘came in/appeared’ is the verb, and به پیش ‘into view / before (them)’ marks direction/location. The verb اندر آمد places the noun forward in the scene: ‘the hem … came forward.’

sentences.s276-s1: And another piece of the demon Niu's armor came forth, with Giv, son of Goudarz.
sentences.s276-s2: They attacked with many spears; they mixed poison with blood.
sentences.s276-s3: The spear-bearer fell from the rider's grasp, plunged down in terror from that battlefield.
sentences.s276-s4: They lifted their bows and (took) dagger‑arrows; one after another they rushed like a leopard.
sentences.s276-s13: He dismounted from the saddle, sent him forward, and went on until he reached his comrade.
sentences.s276-s14: He rose up high with a banner in his hand; with a cry he made the mountain low.
sentences.s276-s15: He shouted for the victory of the king of Iran and repeatedly cried, 'Bravo to the champion!'
sentences.s276-s9: He struck straight down upon his head and his scalp, and blood came from the crown down to his face.
sentences.s276-s10: Hamīdūn took him from the saddle by the hand, seized him tightly to his chest and squeezed him.
sentences.s276-s11: So the man was unseated on the saddle's back; he fell from the horse and became unconscious.
sentences.s276-s12: A warlike panther leapt down; with both hands from behind the back he gripped him like a stone.
sentences.s276-s5: He kept my Giv alive, for he pulled Go‑Niu off his horse.
sentences.s276-s6: Thus bound, he carried it before the king; from the Turks he had brought a certain new gift.
sentences.s276-s7: When Giv entered, startled by the cry, his bow came out of his hand and fell toward the descent.
sentences.s276-s8: At that moment he put his hand toward the sword; the hem of Giv‑Niu came forward into view.
